transcript.transcript: Well, if there's anything else, go ahead and just tell me some of your other things that you've written down. That's fine. Well, I think that's it. Well, let's just see. There was something else I was going to tell you about the question. I forgot what it was. I don't know what it was. Well, one of The first things I can remember was my granddad, and this was 1930, 31. He took me down to the Idaho First national bank building there on 10th and Idaho. And he gave me a silver dollar to start my bank account. And he knew the manager there at the bank and introduced me to him. That made quite an impression. You were only about five years old. Yep. Yep. Just five years old. I can remember all the Chinese gardens. And Mr. Quong had the north end. He'd pull his vegetable wagon up to the north end there, sell them vegetables off the back of his wagon. And then he went from his horses, eventually got an old truck. And he was really proud of that truck when he got it. And was it Mr. Quong, F-O-N-G? No, QUONG, capital Q-U-O-N-G. OK. Mm-hmm. The other big event in the North End in those days, the leaves would all come down. And everybody would rake the leaves up, take them to the gutter, and burn them up. You talked about smoking pooch. You could smell the North End miles away. Every year at the end of the football season, they played in Anthem. And they had a big pep rally up there where Camelback Park is now. And the kids would, all summer long, bring up all the old wood and trees and leaves. And then on the night before the game, they would march from the high school up there, the band would light that bonfire. At that time, not everything was on tour. And sometimes they got a couple outhouses on top of it. Ha ha ha. So they sold outhouses? Yeah. Kip them over and drag them up there. We used to, the kids in the North End, used to sled off of Camel's Back, which we still do now. But the real good place that fled was they had a golf course up where Somerset Hill is. That was the American Legion golf course. Everybody go up there to ball them and fled. Was the American Legion golf course? Pardon? American Legion golf course? American Legion golf course. And that's where you go to fled. They even had a trap shooting range there at that time. The other thing that I can remember, this was the Depression days. And all they had were people that had radios. And not everybody had a radio, either. And the Safeton building was down there on a corner of 6th and Main. And they had the box. Boxing was a big thing at that time. Because they could, one thing they could make money at. And everybody was following Joe Louis and all the boxers. So when they had a big fight, like I remember, the first fight between Joe Lewis and Max Fehman, and Joe Lewis and Billy Kahn, They have a balcony up there on the old Statesman Building. And the printers would take the information off the teletype and then come out and write on the paper, the newspaper roll, and what was going on. There would probably be 400 or 500 people down there in the street watching and reading what was going on. Wow. That was quite a thing. There was a lot of betting going on. And also, at that time, the Bohemian Brewery was on the corner of 6th and Idaho. Corner of 6th and Idaho. And which is 10th corner from that fire station there. And it's picked up about half the block. It was a big brewery. And people would go from the brewery over and watch the news on the site and back and forth. Could you buy it here at the brewery? I couldn't. They had a tap room down there. When I worked down there with the statesmen, The printers at night, they'd take the lunch break, they'd go over to the brewery for the teflon. The brewery was pretty friendly. They would give samples, huh? Yeah. I'm imagining some of those farmhands. And so it was a big deal. But, and then the, one thing that I can remember that was really interesting. In the start of politics, he became a state senator, and he also ran for president. And the progressive party was old William Taylor. And I remember when he was a real good singer, and he could really play the guitar. He was just a heck of an entertainer. And his wife's name was Dora, and they had a little boy by the name of A-rod. And this was before he was in politics, but he kept them and set a big tent up. And sometimes they'd be down there off of Grove Street. And a couple times, they'd be on the vacant lot up in the North End. And they'd put on the tent show. Then I guess they figured that he would do better in politics. I read his book. He has a biography out. It's fascinating. Is that right? Gunther? I'd have to read that. Oh, he was funny. But really, he could sing. And his wife had a beautiful voice. Yes. Then I'll tell you something else you're interested in. The library, where the library is now, they used to be the archival hardware. This is Probably in the late 30s or early 40s, they used to have a coke plant there. And what is it? Coca-Cola? No, coke. Like coal. They bring the coal in, and they burn it, and they get gas. And they pipe the gas up in the North Stand. It was real dangerous. They had a lot of explosions. And that was where the Idaho Hardware Building was. And was it what? But that was prior to the Idaho Hardware going in. Right. Right. They had a lot of explosions. Well, I mean, it was kind of being. So that was right across from the park. Right. Yeah. Right. Wow. Yeah. Yeah. That was. Yeah. Wow. Yeah. I forget what they called it. I think they called it Bush Gap. But I'm not sure. But it was pretty farce when we were kids. It looked bigger than it is. But it was a pretty good-sized installation. And all along the railroad tracks, they'd dump coal. They started dumping coal on the railroad tracks on about 15th Street, clear down, Well, Lewis Coal Yard was right there at the corner of Capitol Boulevard and 10th Street. And during the Depression, people would go down and pick coal up on both sides of the river. The coal really kept it. There wasn't any natural gas, but they burned coal. And so it was the Lewis Coal Yard? It was Lewis Coal Yard. And that was right at Capitol in front. Entrance to the city wasn't that grand, was it? I just love going out to D4 and looking, because it really, if you forget about the mess we made downtown, but just look at the backdrop of the capstone, it hasn't changed much. Right. When I was a young man and a kid, up there on the depot, that was all prune orchard. And they had a couple packing sheds there. And then all along Crescent Rim, they raised hay there in a family by the name of Swishers, who were related to the Swishers, the newspaper man. Uh-huh. Ben Swisher, he had all this ground out there around Mooresville Cemetery. Uh-huh. Around Mooreshill Cemetery. I'll think of the name of the man that had that food business. I know that Stetson had a package shed there. There's a central packing shed there. That's a... And then down below the depot, there was a Chinese that had a lot of gardens down there. There was a Bob's Auto Court on the west side, alongside the old riding ball sleuth. And in the springtime, before the Chad Anderson Dam, the Boise River, in where the college is. And all these floods came in. It was flooded by, so that would be on the east. Actually, where Boise stayed is now is on an island when the river was running on it. It had flowed from the almost closed, the cold hill going up to the... Uh-huh. OK. Yeah. Oh. Uh-huh. Wow. Yeah, it's a... Wow. Wow. Yeah, I think so. You don't remember any floods though at the Julia Davis Park. No, no. Uh-huh. No. No, no, no. Well, I think it washed one of the bridges out there once. But I know it went around where the college was, which was down there on the island. It was just down there. And it's been drilled down off the steep old hill. There's drippy water across Castle Boulevard. It would be water across Castle Boulevard. Yeah, I think you get the... I'll tell you another thing that people don't realize. People don't realize the beauty of space. Before World War II, and I'd say probably, it started as one of the oldest. Ever since Boise Barracks started, I guess they had a calvary unit. And when I was a kid, when the National Guard would come in, I'd stare around the Veterans Hospital. And they're clear over all the hikes in there. Along Fourth Street, there's probably these, probably past 500, 600 horses. Oh, my goodness. And they had a tent camp. Every summer, the tent camp, we would sit in. It would be just east of the VA, and that whole area in there. And the kids, And they were real cowboys. I mean, these kids came from Goody, up north Idaho. They were National Guard. National Guard. And they'd come in the summer. And they'd take their horses and go up the hills. And I remember dragging those little cannons. They used those mountain houses first to drag those cannons up there. And were they doing the new, they were doing training? Yes, they were training. And what did you call the guns? Howitzers, mountain howitzers. Now, how it says mountain, how it says peak. And we found that Gibson North End, not all of them were the ones that were trying to make a little money. We'd go up and they had inspections every Saturday. And on Friday night and Saturday morning, we'd go up there and we'd have little shoebox kits and we'd shine their shoes. And we'd get 5 or 10 cents for shining their boots. And it was really crazy. We'd walk all through that tent. There was no security. And go by the mess hall and if you're hungry, if they had any cookies or anything, they'd give it to you. And Those soldiers were real cowboys. I remember that. They were good, strong tough kids. What did they think? Well, they had a lot of stables over there. So did the horses stay? No. I was trying to think how they got those horses in, because they didn't truck them. There wasn't any trucking. I don't know whether they brought them in in rail cars. But they pastured. I know that the units in Nant and Caldwell, they probably brought their own. But these kids from Goody and Jerome and Eastern Idaho, I don't know where they got to. But they all, they, they, they. I'll tell you, this is really shady about the horse. John Berencher. John Berencher? John Berensher. He took care of me. He graduated. Of course, he went to Corsi High. And I think he was a year ahead of me. But he's still alive. But he was very active in the horse business. He took care of all the pole, as Dad did, the pole horses for the Fletchers and the Goodmans. Did people ride horses down at Julia Davis Park? Pardon me? Did they ride horses at Julia Davis Park? I didn't see any. No. No. I've seen a lot of dogs. They weren't on leash, I bet. No. But no, I've seen horses. Well, the other thing that's kind of helpful that I forget is that they had this beautiful clay tennis court there at the Sweet Edith Park. And that's where Boise High and Fox practiced playing all their tennis matches. And then, oh, yeah. We'd go down there and play tennis. Uh-huh. Uh-huh. They were clay courts, weren't they? Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. Oh, they were nice courts. Yeah. They're just redoing them now. Mm-hmm. Right. Right. They're coming out of Utah. Oh, is that right? Mm-hmm. I hope they do better. And They're not going to be played all this way. I think you told me they're going to be asked for. Uh-huh. Yeah. Mm-hmm. Let's see. Well, I'll take it. You've covered it? Pardon? You've covered it? Well, I'll just show you one more of those things. And then there's the up there at the mandatorium. I remember the old mast. Yeah. The one that had the roof on it. Uh-huh. That was a beautiful, old, old structure. Yeah. You'd swim there? Oh, yeah. Yeah. Yeah, And then they had White City Park, where they had the roller coasters. White City Park? Mm-hmm. White City Park. And was that right there? Mm-hmm, right. When you were a little boy, were you ever aware that it was dangerous to be at the park? Or were there any... No. No? No. It's all safe? No. Never worried about anything. What about when your children were growing up and you went to the park in the 50s? Your children were born in the 50s. Well, we walked them around the river once. No, we never worried about it. OK. Yeah. Did you go to the Pete Seeger concert that was there? Did you go to the Pete Seeger concert? No. No. No. No. Do you remember about it? It's quite controversial. Right. You know, that's the thing is, everything's controversial. You know, when I was growing up, things were pretty peaceful. It's getting very polarized. Yeah. Yeah. Well, you have great memories. That's why I never left Boise. I just love Boise. I love St. Idol. When I was in the Navy, I couldn't wait to get home. You know, as you're growing up, it's real easy. I could have done a better job, but I didn't want to. Didn't want to leave? No. Good. Well, thank you so much. And thank you also for giving the history that your father wrote. That is a very precious gift that will be at the Historical Library for researchers and others to see. So your father was born in 1898. 97. 97. So, really appreciate the gift. Thank you. Is that it? Yep. Okay. Thank you. Thank you.

story.title: Jones_Robert_P_OH 2705 Side B
story.description: Recorded on February 15, 2007. Topic is Julia Davis Park.
